skill.name: cloud-native
skill.description: Cloud-native deployment and infrastructure patterns for Infrastructure as Code, GitOps, progressive delivery, multi-cloud operations, and platform engineering.
skill.summary: Design cloud-native infrastructure, GitOps workflows, and progressive delivery patterns across providers.
skill.author: Azeem-2

security_audit.summary: The skill is not confirmed malicious and no prompt injection was found, but it includes high-risk operational examples. The remote shell installation pattern and shell-executed deployment hooks are confirmed; many weak-crypto, dynamic-import, and reconnaissance hits are false positives from Markdown fences, provider names, timestamps, and ordinary code examples.

high_findings.title: Remote Shell Installation Pattern
high_findings.description: The Flux installation example builds a command containing a remote install script URL and pipes it to bash through shell execution. This pattern can execute changed remote content with the privileges of the current user.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code explicitly combines a remote install script with a pipe to bash and then calls the command with shell=True. This is a confirmed dangerous pattern, although it appears instructional rather than malicious.

high_findings.title: Deployment Hooks Executed Through Bash
high_findings.description: Deployment hook values from application configuration are passed to a shell wrapper. If hook content comes from an untrusted repository or user, it can execute arbitrary commands.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hook string is read from app.hooks and executed with shell=True, then converted to bash -c. The injection risk depends on who controls hook configuration, but the execution behavior is clear.

medium_findings.title: Auto-Approved Infrastructure Changes
medium_findings.description: Terraform and Pulumi examples apply or destroy infrastructure with automatic approval flags. This can cause destructive changes without a required human confirmation gate.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands use -auto-approve or --yes for apply and destroy operations. This is a real operational risk, but it is common in controlled CI pipelines.

medium_findings.title: Generated Pulumi Code Uses Config Values Directly
medium_findings.description: The Pulumi generator writes Python source by interpolating resource names, property names, and values. Untrusted configuration could create invalid or unintended Python code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The generator concatenates config-derived values into Python source before writing __main__.py. The risk is strong when configuration is untrusted, but the skill does not show an external input source.

low_findings.title: Infrastructure Files Written To Working Directory
low_findings.description: The Terraform generator writes main.tf, variables.tf, and outputs.tf into the selected working directory. This is expected for IaC generation but can overwrite existing files if used carelessly.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are explicit and expected for the skill domain. The main concern is overwrite behavior, not malicious intent.

low_findings.title: External Manifest Dependency
low_findings.description: The Argo CD initialization example applies a Kubernetes manifest directly from a GitHub URL. This is a supply-chain dependency but not evidence of exfiltration or malware.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL points to the Argo CD project manifest and is used by kubectl apply. It is a legitimate pattern with supply-chain risk if the remote content changes.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives
low_findings.description: The dynamic import, weak cryptography, and system reconnaissance alerts are not confirmed threats in context. The cited locations are Markdown fences, relative imports, provider source names, timestamps, default Kubernetes service URLs, or normal loop variables.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found no weak cryptographic algorithm use, dynamic runtime import, or reconnaissance behavior at these representative locations. The matches are caused by benign text or ordinary example code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Shell Installation Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The Flux installation example builds a command containing a remote install script URL and pipes it to bash through shell execution. This pattern can execute changed remote content with the privileges of the current user.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code explicitly combines a remote install script with a pipe to bash and then calls the command with shell=True. This is a confirmed dangerous pattern, although it appears instructional rather than malicious.

dangerous_patterns.title: Deployment Hooks Executed Through Bash
dangerous_patterns.description: Deployment hook values from application configuration are passed to a shell wrapper. If hook content comes from an untrusted repository or user, it can execute arbitrary commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The hook string is read from app.hooks and executed with shell=True, then converted to bash -c. The injection risk depends on who controls hook configuration, but the execution behavior is clear.

dangerous_patterns.title: Auto-Approved Infrastructure Changes
dangerous_patterns.description: Terraform and Pulumi examples apply or destroy infrastructure with automatic approval flags. This can cause destructive changes without a required human confirmation gate.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands use -auto-approve or --yes for apply and destroy operations. This is a real operational risk, but it is common in controlled CI pipelines.

content.value_statement: Cloud teams need repeatable patterns for infrastructure, delivery, and platform operations. This skill provides provider-agnostic guidance for IaC, GitOps, canary delivery, and multi-cloud workflows.
actual_capabilities: Provides Terraform, Pulumi, and CDK-style abstraction patterns for infrastructure workflows.
Outlines GitOps engine concepts for Argo CD, Flux, and deployment synchronization.
Describes multi-cloud resource management with priority, failover, and provider selection patterns.
Shows canary analysis and traffic-splitting structures for progressive delivery.
Includes examples for generating IaC files and running deployment tool commands.
limitations: The examples are reference patterns and require security hardening before production use.
The skill does not validate provider credentials, cloud account permissions, or policy constraints.
Some examples assume Kubernetes, Terraform, Pulumi, or GitOps tools are already installed.
Remote script installation and shell hook examples should be replaced with safer installation methods.

use_cases.title: Standardize IaC Patterns
use_cases.description: Create reusable infrastructure abstractions for Terraform or Pulumi across teams and environments.

use_cases.title: Design GitOps Delivery
use_cases.description: Plan application synchronization, health checks, and rollout automation using Argo CD or Flux concepts.

use_cases.title: Plan Safer Releases
use_cases.description: Model canary delivery, rollback triggers, and traffic shifting for production services.

prompt_templates.title: Map My Cloud-Native Needs
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my application, team size, cloud provider, and deployment goals. Recommend a simple cloud-native architecture and explain the tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Create an IaC Structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an Infrastructure as Code folder structure for my service. Include environments, modules, variables, outputs, and validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Plan GitOps Rollouts
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a GitOps rollout plan for this Kubernetes application. Include sync policy, health checks, promotion gates, and rollback handling.

prompt_templates.title: Harden a Multi-Cloud Platform
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this multi-cloud deployment design for reliability, security, cost control, and operational risk. Recommend concrete changes and guardrails.

output_examples.input: A team wants to move manual Kubernetes deployments into GitOps.
output_examples.output: A phased migration plan with repository layout, environment promotion flow, sync rules, health checks, and rollback checkpoints.

output_examples.input: A platform group needs common IaC patterns for three cloud providers.
output_examples.output: A provider-neutral abstraction outline, module boundaries, state handling guidance, and validation requirements.

output_examples.input: An SRE wants canary releases with automatic rollback.
output_examples.output: A canary strategy with traffic steps, metric thresholds, analysis windows, and failure actions.

best_practices: Review generated infrastructure changes before apply or destroy commands run.
Pin external manifests and installers to trusted versions with checksum verification.
Keep deployment hooks explicit, reviewed, and limited to trusted repositories.
anti_patterns: Do not pipe remote install scripts directly into a shell in production automation.
Do not run auto-approved destroy operations without environment and approval controls.
Do not generate executable code from untrusted configuration without validation.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps design cloud-native infrastructure, GitOps workflows, multi-cloud operations, and progressive delivery patterns.

faq.question: Is this skill tied to one cloud provider?
faq.answer: No. The examples are provider-agnostic and mention AWS, Azure, Google Cloud, DigitalOcean, and on-premises environments.

faq.question: Can I use the examples directly in production?
faq.answer: Use them as starting points only. Add validation, approvals, version pinning, and organization-specific security controls first.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk high?
faq.answer: The skill includes remote script execution through a shell and hook execution through bash, which are dangerous patterns.

faq.question: Does the audit show malicious intent?
faq.answer: No evidence of malware, exfiltration, or prompt injection was found during review.

faq.question: Which AI tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
